answer:
**Reflection on language in the Falsafa corpus occurs primarily in three contexts: poetic verses that contemplate the power, limits, or essence of speech and words; legal smṛti texts that prescribe rules for proper or improper speech (e.g., harshness, truthfulness, or clarity); and philosophical prose addressing freedom of expression and thought.**

**No works feature systematic philosophy of language** (e.g., etymology, semantics, or ontology of names/words akin to Plato's *Cratylus*). The 37-work corpus emphasizes ethics, law, devotion, and politics over linguistics. Iqbal's Urdu poetry offers the most overt poetic reflections; Anglo-Saxon verse implies it through kennings like "word-hoard" (eloquence/vocabulary); Indic smṛtis treat speech pragmatically (right/wrong use); Fichte explicitly defends communicative freedom.[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5]

Representative passages:

- **Bang-E-Dara Part 3** (Allama Iqbal): Mankind is proud of its own gift of speech, / Yet how to speak is far beyond its reach.[^6]

- **Diwan-E-Ghalib** (Mirza Ghalib): Let consciousness set out its net of hearing far and wide; / The meaning of my world of words is a phantom bird I speak of.[^7]

- **Andreas** (Cynewulf): [The speaker] began to speak then, shrewd in his counsel, / wise in his wits, he unlocked his word-hoard: / “Never have I met a better seaman... / wiser in his words...”[^8]

- **Kātyāyana Smṛti**: When one reviles another with terms denoting non-existent or existent defects in his limbs, that speech is remembered by the wise as harsh.[^9]

- **Bṛhaspati Smṛti**: Conciliation is speech preceded by kindness; sowing dissension is showing fear...[^10]

[^1]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [work card](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/wiki/_work.card.md).

[^2]: *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [work card](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/wiki/_work.card.md).

[^3]: *Andreas*, [work card](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/wiki/_work.card.md); cf. repeated "unlocked his word-hoard" motif.

[^4]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, ch. 65, [work card](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/wiki/_work.card.md).

[^5]: *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit*, ch. 1: critiques "declamation" vs. thorough speech, demands "freedom of thought" against suppression.[^11]

[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 4, [p-5e0e63](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-5e0e63).

[^7]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 153, [p-8cc477](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/153-naqsh-faryadi-hai-kis-ki-shokhi-e-tehreer-ka/translation/#p-8cc477).

[^8]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, ch. 1, [p-ca9772](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-ca9772).

[^9]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, ch. 65, [p-78680d](/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/65/translation/#p-78680d).

[^10]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, ch. 1, [p-9ce460](/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-9ce460).

[^11]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit*, ch. 1, [chapter](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/01/translation/).
